title: Dog Wart Bleeding on Lip: Causes, Urgency, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Find out why your dog's lip wart is bleeding. Learn about canine papillomas, urgency levels, and why a photo helps your vet with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Warty lip growths are usually benign canine papillomas and are rarely an emergency even if they bleed. Document the growth with photos and schedule a non-urgent veterinary visit for a formal diagnosis.

What is the most common cause of a wart-like growth on my dog's lip?

A growth on a dog's lip that looks like a wart is most commonly a canine papilloma. These are benign tumors caused by the canine papillomavirus. They often have a distinct 'cauliflower' or fimbriated appearance and are frequently found in or around the mouth of younger dogs or those with slightly compromised immune systems.

Is it a veterinary emergency if the growth on my dog's lip begins to bleed?

  • The urgency level for a bleeding lip growth is typically low, as these masses are highly vascular and located in areas prone to friction from teeth or toys.
  • Unless the bleeding is profuse and cannot be stopped with gentle pressure, it is usually not a life-threatening situation.
  • Any new or changing growth should still be evaluated by a veterinarian during a scheduled appointment to confirm it is benign.

How does taking a photo of the growth help my veterinarian with triage?

  • Allows the clinical team to assess the shape, color, and size of the mass before your appointment.
  • Provides a vital visual baseline to track if the wart is growing, shrinking, or changing in texture over time.
  • Helps determine the necessity of surgical removal or further diagnostic testing based on the appearance of the lesion.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Oral papillomas in dogs can present as growths on the lips and oral mucous membranes, occasionally causing bleeding if traumatized during chewing due to their location and size. These papillomas are typically caused by a virus, and while they often regress spontaneously within a few weeks to months, azithromycin therapy may speed up regression. Surgical removal can be considered if the growths interfere with prehension, mastication, or swallowing, or if bleeding is persistent; debulking can be achieved via electro- or radiosurgery or sharp resection. Severe oral papillomatosis can also be seen in immunocompromised dogs. While less common, other wart-like lesions may be benign exophytic proliferations that are generally slow growing and solitary; surgical removal is often curative for these.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 366)

faqs.question: Can I catch warts from my dog?
faqs.answer: No. Canine papillomavirus is species-specific, meaning it only spreads between dogs and cannot be transmitted to humans or other types of pets.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: You can apply gentle, steady pressure to the area with a clean cloth or gauze for 5 to 10 minutes. Avoid wiping, as this can dislodge clots and cause bleeding to resume.

faqs.question: Do dog warts require surgery?
faqs.answer: Most canine warts resolve on their own within a few months as the dog's immune system matures. Surgery is usually only recommended if the wart is causing pain, difficulty eating, or persistent bleeding.
